name: Morgellons Disease
description: Morgellons disease is a controversial and poorly understood condition primarily self-diagnosed by individuals who report a range of cutaneous (skin) symptoms, including crawling, biting, and stinging sensations; the presence of fibers, filaments, or other materials emerging from the skin; and skin lesions. Some patients also report systemic symptoms such as fatigue, cognitive dysfunction, and musculoskeletal pain. The term "Morgellons" was initially used in the 17th century to describe a similar condition in French children. The modern usage originated in 2002 when Mary Leitao coined the term to describe her son's symptoms. Proponents believe Morgellons is a distinct, previously unrecognized disease, possibly caused by an infectious agent, nanotechnology, or environmental toxins. They often cite microscopic analysis of skin samples revealing unusual fibers and particles as evidence. Mainstream medical science generally considers Morgellons to be a somatic delusional disorder, a psychiatric condition where individuals have fixed, false beliefs about their physical health. Studies, including a large one by the CDC, have found no evidence of an infectious cause or a novel pathogen. The CDC study suggested that the fibers found in skin samples were most likely textile fibers. The debate continues, with patient advocacy groups pushing for further research and recognition of Morgellons as a legitimate medical condition while the majority of the medical community attributes the symptoms to underlying psychiatric or medical conditions, often exacerbated by excessive scratching and picking.
subtitle: A contested skin condition with disputed origins

claims.claim: Morgellons is a distinct, unrecognized disease.
claims.evidence: Reports of specific symptoms like crawling sensations, fibers emerging from the skin, and debilitating fatigue.
claims.criticism: Mainstream medicine classifies it as a somatic delusion or a manifestation of underlying medical/psychiatric conditions. No consistent objective biomarkers have been identified.

claims.claim: Fibers and other unusual materials are found in the skin of Morgellons patients.
claims.evidence: Microscopic analysis of skin samples purportedly reveals colored fibers, granules, and other foreign objects.
claims.criticism: Studies have shown these fibers are often textile fibers or other environmental contaminants. No evidence of a unique or infectious agent has been found.

claims.claim: Morgellons is caused by an infectious agent, nanotechnology, or environmental toxins.
claims.evidence: The specific cause is unknown to proponents, but various theories are proposed, including Lyme disease co-infections, genetically modified organisms, and chemtrails.
claims.criticism: Extensive research, including CDC studies, has failed to identify any infectious agent or environmental toxin consistently associated with Morgellons. The nanotechnology claim lacks scientific basis.

keyFigures.name: Mary Leitao
keyFigures.role: Coined the term 'Morgellons' in modern usage and founded the Morgellons Research Foundation.

keyFigures.name: Michele Pearson
keyFigures.role: Lead author of the CDC study on Morgellons disease.

keyFigures.name: Randy Wymore
keyFigures.role: Pharmacology professor who advocated for Morgellons research and believed in its organic basis.

mythologyConnections: Possible parallels with folklore about skin ailments and curses
Connections to stories about parasitic infestations and demonic possession

timeline.event: Term 'Morgellons' used in France to describe a similar condition in children.
Mary Leitao coins the term 'Morgellons' for her son's symptoms.
The Morgellons Research Foundation (MRF) is founded.
CDC begins a study to investigate Morgellons disease.
CDC publishes its findings, concluding that Morgellons is not a new disease and is likely a somatic delusional disorder.

sources.author: Pearson, M.L. et al.
sources.work: Clinical, Epidemiologic, Histopathologic and Molecular Features of Morgellons Disease
sources.title: Pearson, M.L. et al.
